questions.stem: A 7-year-old biological male is brought to a psychologist by concerned parents. The child consistently insists on wearing dresses, prefers playing with dolls, exclusively chooses female playmates, and has expressed a strong desire to be a girl for the past 8 months. According to DSM-5-TR criteria, how many of the specified symptoms must be present to diagnose gender dysphoria in children?
options: At least 2 of 6 specified symptoms for 6 or more months
At least 4 of 8 specified symptoms for 6 or more months
At least 6 of 8 specified symptoms for 6 or more months
At least 3 of 5 specified symptoms for 12 or more months
questions.answer: At least 6 of 8 specified symptoms for 6 or more months
questions.explanation: The DSM-5-TR requires children to meet 6 of 8 specified symptoms for at least 6 months for a diagnosis of gender dysphoria. This threshold is higher than for adolescents/adults, who need only 2 of 6 symptoms, because children express gender identity through observable behaviors (toy preferences, clothing choices, playmate selection) rather than through the more articulate self-awareness available to older individuals. Option A (2 of 6) describes the adolescent/adult criteria. Options B and D are fabricated thresholds.
questions.kn_explanation: Question involves diagnosing Gender Dysphoria in a child using DSM classification criteria, fitting classification systems and evaluation of client functioning.
questions.quality_comment: Directly tests a high-yield diagnostic threshold distinction between child and adult criteria that commonly appears on the EPPP.
questions.lessonExcerpt: **For Children** (need 6 of 8 symptoms for 6+ months): Strong desire to be another gender, Strong preference for cross-gender clothing, Preference for cross-gender roles in play, Preference for toys/activities typical of other gender, Preference for playmates of other gender, Rejection of own anatomy, Strong desire for physical sex characteristics of other gender, General distress about assigned gender
